coredata.dc:title: Bright hydrophilic and organophilic fluorescence carbon dots: One-pot fabrication and multi-functional applications at visualized Au3+ detection in cell and white light-emitting devices
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Wenli
Zhou, Yanmei
Xu, Chenggong
Guo, Meixia
Qi, Zhichong
Peng, Xiaojun
Gao, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Hydrophilic and organophilic carbon dots (HCDs and OCDs) have been synthesized simultaneously by simple one-pot solvothermal carbonization of methylene blue and sulfuric acid using ethanol as the solvent. The synthesized HCDs and OCDs show evidently different properties, which can be respectively dissolved in distilled water and ethanol, exhibiting bright white and blue fluorescence under an UV illumination of 365 nm, and their fluorescence quantum yield reach up to 16.5% and 21.1%, respectively. The different functional groups on the surface are responsible for the distinction in fluorescence and solubility of these two kinds of carbon dots. Especially, HCDs can not only be used in visualizing detection of Au3+ in PC12 cells, but also fabricating the white-light-emitting diodes, implying their potential in exploiting low-cost CDs as replacements of phosphors for light-emitting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophilic carbon dots
Organophilic carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Au3+ biosensing
White light-emitting devices

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and optical properties of the HCDs and OCDs
Mechanism for the formation of HCDs and OCDs
Sensitive and selective detection of Au3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (insets are size distribution histograms and HRTEM) of HCDs (a) and OCDs (b); AFM images and the height analysis (insets) along the corresponding lines of HCDs (c) and OCDs (d).
FT-IR spectra (a) and full survey XPS spectra (b) of the HCDs and OCDs. (c–d) High resolution spectra of O1s peaks of HCDs and OCDs. (e–f) High-resolution spectra of the C1s peaks of HCDs and OCDs.

PL emission spectra of HCDs (a) and OCDs (b). Insert: photographs of the HCDs in water (a) and OCDs in ethanol (b) under 365 nm UV light irradiation. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of OCDs and HCDs in the absence and presence of Au3+ solution.

(a) Fluorescence intensity changes (F/F0) of the HCDs solution with and without of various metal ions. The columns in “Selectivity” system is stand for the fluorescence response of HCDs to the metal ions. The columns in “Competition” group express the succeeding addition of 400 μM Au3+ to the corresponding solutions. (b) The relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and concentration of Au3+ within the range of 0–400 μM, two linear correlations of (F0-F)/F0 values versus the concentration of Au3+ over the range from 0 to 80 μM and 80 to 400 μM, respectively.

LSCM images of PC12 cells. Bright-field image (a1–e1); fluorescence image of the HCDs in PC12 cells treated with the diverse concentrations of Au3+ (0, 100, 200, 300, 400 μM) (a2–e2); merge image (a3–e3). The cells were collected at emission of 450–700 nm for green channel upon excitation with light laser at 458 nm.
(a) The emission spectra of UV-LED with HCDs/PVA composite coating. Inset: photograph of the WLED. (b) CIE color coordinate of the WLED.
XPS elemental analysis results, quantum yields and fluorescent lifetimes of OCDs and HCDs, as well as the fluorescent lifetime of HCDs with Au3+.

head.ce:title: Bright hydrophilic and organophilic fluorescence carbon dots: One-pot fabrication and multi-functional applications at visualized Au3+ detection in cell and white light-emitting devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng, Henan 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Agricultural and Biological Engineering, University of Florida, Gainesville, FL 32611, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Agricultural and Biological Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng, Henan 475004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrophilic and organophilic carbon dots (HCDs and OCDs) have been synthesized simultaneously by simple one-pot solvothermal carbonization of methylene blue and sulfuric acid using ethanol as the solvent. The synthesized HCDs and OCDs show evidently different properties, which can be respectively dissolved in distilled water and ethanol, exhibiting bright white and blue fluorescence under an UV illumination of 365 nm, and their fluorescence quantum yield reach up to 16.5% and 21.1%, respectively. The different functional groups on the surface are responsible for the distinction in fluorescence and solubility of these two kinds of carbon dots. Especially, HCDs can not only be used in visualizing detection of Au3+ in PC12 cells, but also fabricating the white-light-emitting diodes, implying their potential in exploiting low-cost CDs as replacements of phosphors for light-emitting devices.

ce:section.ce:para: As a rising star of carbon materials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their excellent features such as outstanding optical properties, good photostability, and low toxicity. CDs have been potential applied in the fields of sensing, bioimaging, drug carriers, photocatalysis, photovoltaic devices, and solar cells [1–9]. Most synthetic CDs show hydrophilicity in nature and have always been the concern of researchers. For example, Qu et al. reported preparation of water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots by microwave route, which can be used as biocompatible fluorescent ink for versatile applications [10]. Di. and co-workers synthesized carbon quantum dots with great water solubility and that can be used for sensitive detection of arsenite [11]. Meanwhile, the organophilic carbon dots (OCDs) are also attracting more and more attention in recent works owing to their applications in electronic devices and drug delivery [12,13]. Some methods have been proposed for the synthesis of OCDs, including the way of hydrophobic long-chain organic molecules as surface passivation agents, and the method of functional molecules modification to hydrophilic carbon dots (HCDs) surface [12,14]. While it is rare to synthesize HCDs and OCDs directly with high fluorescent at the same time by facile one-pot and low-cost synthetic methods [12,13,15–17].

The application of CDs in the aspect of sensing metal ions has been reported extensively, while most of them were used for detection of Fe3+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ [6,18], and there are few reports about using CDs as fluorescent probe for high sensitive and selective sensing Au3+ [19–22]. Gold, as a type of noble metal, plays an important role in many fields, including catalysis, biology and medicine. Nevertheless, based on the remarkable increase of gold catalysis and gold nanoparticles, the toxicity that is related with Au3+ also increase remarkably, and it has been found to potential biotoxicity [23]. For instance, the Au3+ can bind tightly to several enzymes and DNA, and can lead to damage to the liver, kidneys and peripheral nervous system [19]. Accordingly, it is quite important to sense Au3+ in the biological systems. In our previous work, we have been synthesized the CDs for detection of Au3+ in aqueous solution and bioimaging in cell [21]. However, as far as we known, it has not been reported that noble metal ions of Au3+ can be visualized detection in cell using the CDs.

The fluorescent CDs had been applied to production of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) because of their less toxicity, tunable stable ﬂuorescent emission and low cost [24]. As we all know, white light can be generated from dichromatic type of blue and yellow light or through an association of trichromatic type of blue, green, and red light [25]. White-light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) are a new generation of green illuminant owing to their long-lifetime capabilities, greater efficiency and environment friendly. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have drawn rising scrutiny in the field of WLEDs because of their adjustable emission, high quantum yield, and narrow emission bandwidth [25,26]. Nevertheless, traditional QDs generally include toxic heavy metals that this exist toxic and detrimental effects to the environment and humans, which will restrict their development. Therefore, it will be extremely meaningful to develop new materials with low toxicity and low cost to replace the QDs application in LEDs [26,27].

To solve the above-mentioned issues associated with gaining CDs with different features, and to achieve multi-functional applications of CDs, we prepared HCDs and OCDs at the same time by one-pot solvothermal treating methylene blue (MB) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) in ethanol solution. The two kinds of CDs showed excitation-dependent emission property, and high fluorescent quantum yields (QYs) of 16.5% (HCDs) and 21.1% (OCDs). As-synthesized HCDs were used as highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probe for sensing Au3+. Meanwhile, the HCDs possessed wonderful biocompatibility, which could be used for visualizing detection of Au3+ in PC12 cells. As far as we known, the prepared HCDs in this research stand for the first CDs-based probe that could be applied for the visual detection of Au3+ in cells. Furthermore, the HCDs could be employed for the fabrication of the WLEDs.
All materials and characterizations were displayed in the supplementary material.

Optimization of synthetic conditions was shown in the supplementary material. In a typical preparation procedure, 8 mg MB and 0.1 mL H2SO4 were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol, stirred for one minute to make homogeneous mixture solution. Then the mixture solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The black lower solution (HCDs) and red upper liquid (OCDs) were obtained. In order to gain the pure HCDs and OCDs, lower and upper liquids were filtered by the 0.22 μm filter membranes.

Briefly, 50 μL synthesized HCDs solution was added into 3 mL of distilled water, the fluorescence spectrum of HCDs aqueous solution was recorded under the excitation of 440 nm. Then various concentrations of Au3+ (0–400 μM) were added to 3 mL of the above HCDs solution. After half a minute for equilibration, the fluorescence spectra of each sample were recorded. The selectivity towards Au3+ was measured by adding other metal ions (including Ag+, Pd2+, Pt2+, Rh3+, Ir3+, Ru3+, Os4+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+ and Ni+), and for the ions competition experiments, the 400 μM Au3+ was subsequently added to the respective solutions with metals ions. A suite of F/F0 values (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of the HCDs corresponding to the existence and absence of Au3+) were gained.
The experiment of cell imaging was described in the supplementary material.

First, fluorescent HCDs/Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite was fabricated as follows: typically, 1 g of PVA was dispersed into 7 mL HCDs aqueous solution at 85 °C for 1 h under vigorous stirring. UV-LED chip with the emission wavelength of 365 nm was chosen for the fabrication of WLEDs. Then, 30 μL HCDs/PVA composite was coated on the optical lens of the bottom of the chip and heating cured the samples at 60 °C for 1 h. Finally, WLEDs were produced by coupling the HCDs/PVA composite films with UV-LED chip.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of the HCDs and OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties, morphology and structure of the as-prepared HCDs and OCDs were characterized. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images illustrated that the HCDs and OCDs exhibited well distribution, and their average size were about 5.5 nm and 9 nm, respectively. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of HCDs (inset in Fig. 1a) showed a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, similar to that of the graphite (100) facet [6]. A lattice spacing of 0.36 nm was displayed in the HRTEM image of OCDs (inset in Fig. 1b), corresponding to the plan of graphite carbon (002) [7]. The result of atomic force microscope (AFM) images in Fig. 1c and d showed that the average height of the HCDs and OCDs were about 5.5 nm and 9.1 nm, which were coincided well with their results of TEM, revealing that the synthesized CDs are nearly spherical morphologies. Fig. S1 presented X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the two kinds of CDs, the wide peaks diffraction peaks (2θ) of the HCDs and OCDs were centered around 24° and 42°, corresponding to C(002) and C(100) planes of typical graphitic structure [28,29]. As shown in Fig. S2, both of them exhibited typical Raman spectrum of CDs with broad peaks. And it can be observed that the disordered (D) band of OCDs was weaker than the crystalline (G) band with an intensity ratio of 0.402, obviously smaller than that of HCDs (I D /I G = 1.01). This result suggested that HCDs have a lower degree of graphitization and correspondingly more defects or disorder structures [30].

The two kinds of CDs were characterized by using the fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra so as to confirm their chemical property. In Fig. 2 a, the peaks at 1642 cm−1 and 1427 cm−1 could be observed, corresponding to CO/CN and CN bonds [26,31]. The stretching vibrations absorption at 1181 cm-1 is attributed to the CN bonds [32]. The peaks of two kinds of CDs at 1034 and 1159 cm−1 are because of the SO/COC and CS bond vibrations, respectively [33]. And the peak at 871 cm−1 is ascribed to the CCS structure [34]. It is noteworthy that, the typical hydroxy peak at 3397 cm−1 is evidently viewed from HCDs while there is no visible consistent peak on the OCDs. Furthermore, the obviously stretching vibration of CH3 and CH2 of the OCDs are observed at about 2897 and 2982 cm−1, respectively [13,35]. These results indicated that the major distinction between HCDs and OCDs was determined by the content and genre of the oxygen interrelated groups on their surfaces. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) full spectra of HCDs and OCDs in Fig. 2b showed four peaks about at 532.1 eV, 401.9 eV, 284.6 eV and 168.9 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s and S2p. The contents of atom for four elements were shown in Table 1 . The contents of oxygen in HCDs and OCDs are 42.93% and 28.45%, respectively. The comparison of high resolution O1s spectra in Fig. 2c and d confirmed the distinct difference in chemical compositions for 532.76 eV are attributed to CO, COH and COC groups [36]. The OCDs displayed two peaks at 530.94 eV (CO) and 532.46 eV (COC). The results of XPS data are consistent with their FT-IR spectra. The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2e revealed that the HCDs contain CC, CC, CS, CO, CO, and CN groups, Fig. 2f indicated that the OCDs exist CO, CS, CC, CC, and CO groups [12,19,37,38]. The above characterizations indicated that HCDs had more hydrophilic functional group with respect to OCDs, and the various functional groups on the surface determine the difference in solubility and properties of two kinds of CDs. Table 1 showed that carbon content of OCDs was 67.78%, dramatically higher than that of HCDs (37.42%).

Fig. S3 showed the optical properties of HCDs and OCDs. Specifically, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of HCDs exhibited a peak at 243 nm, attributed to π→π* transition of the CC [14]. For the OCDs, a stronger absorption peak at 267 nm was observed, which demonstrated OCDs exhibited more conjugated aromatic structure [12,39]. The excitation wavelength dependent emission spectra of two kinds of CDs were shown in Fig. 3 a and b, HCDs revealed wide emission bands under excitations from 300 to 380 nm, with two primary chromatic components that peaked at 455 nm (blue) and 530 nm (yellow). In the inset of Fig. 3a, displaying HCDs aqueous solution possess the white fluorescence at the UV light irradiation of 365 nm. For the photoluminescent (PL) spectrum of OCDs, Fig. 3b showed the redshift of the fluorescent maximum from 375 to 590 nm with excitation wavelengths from 320 to 500 nm. The images of OCDs solution at 365 nm UV light irradiation were inserted in Fig. 3b, demonstrating a bright blue fluorescence. The maximum emission wavelengths of the HCDs and OCDs were 515 and 455 nm under the excitation wavelengths of 440 and 360 nm. Their PL spectra presented that there were diverse PL properties between HCDs and OCDs. The PL QYs of HCDs and OCDs are 16.5% and 21.1% with fluorescein and quinine sulfate as the standard when excited at 440 and 360 nm, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) decay curves of the two types of CDs were shown in Fig. 3c, the results illustrated that their τ decay curves were optimum fitted to a monoexponential function, and HCDs displays a longer τ than OCDs (in Table 1). The single exponential decay features illustrate that the excitons are greatly steady and do not find non-radiative emission channels inside the HCDs and OCDs, which are beneficial to valid ﬂuorescence emission and obviously different from reported CDs with multi-exponential decay [23]. The τ of CDs being in the nanosecond regime explains that prepared CDs are ideal for use in biological and optoelectronic fields [21,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the formation of HCDs and OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: During the hydrothermal process, the chemical structures of the precursors undergo complicated transformations and condensation [12]. The formation mechanism of HCDs is likely due to that the hydroxyl groups undergo dehydration, subsequently, polymerization yields water soluble polymers beyond 100 ◦C, the polymers undergo carbonization/aromatization by condensation and cycloaddition due to the high catenation ability of carbon [29,41]. As previously describe that HCDs and OCDs are gained at the same time. The yields of HCDs and OCDs were further researched by increasing reaction time. With the reaction time increase, the yield of OCDs increased obviously and that for HCDs decreased. These results showed that HCDs could go through further carbonization to form OCDs [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Au3+
ce:section.ce:para: It is relatively significant to detect noble metal ions of Au3+ because of its expensiveness and toxicity. To study the fluorescence sensing of the HCDs, the fluorescence response of the HCDs was investigated in the presence of diverse metal ions, which were shown in Fig. 4 a. The F/F0 of the HCDs could be distinctly quenched only in the presence of Au3+, and there were small or no change of F/F0 of HCDs after adding other metal ions. Fig. 4a presented that F/F0 of HCDs towards Au3+ was almost not be affected by other metal ions. The results indicated that the HCDs had a good ion selectivity and anti-interference ability which can be used as sensitive and selective fluorescent probe for sensing Au3+. The relationship between the quenching ratio (F0-F)/F0 and concentrations of Au3+ was displayed in Fig. 4b, which showed two good linear correlations with Au3+ concentration in the range of 0–80 μM and 80–400 μM, corresponding to the correlation efficient (R2) of 0.9905 and 0.9914. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 3.35 μM ground on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD = 3δ/K, δ represents the standard deviation for the blank measurements, K is the slope of the calibration curve) [21].

For explaining the fluorescence quenching process of HCDs, the PL τ was researched. It can be seen in Fig. 3c that there is no distinct changed in values of τ (in Table 1) without and with the presence of 400 μM Au3+ when excited with 445 nm laser and emission measured at 515 nm, proving the inner filter effect (IFE) occurring between HCDs and Au3+ [42]. In order to further confirm the detection mechanism, UV–vis absorption spectra of HCDs before and after adding Au3+ were recorded. As shown in Fig. S3, the adsorption band of HCDs was decreased obviously in the presence of Au3+, illustrating it happened the IFE between Au3+ and HCDs resulting in the fluorescence quenching effect [43]. The fluorescent quenching mechanism by Au3+ was further studied. Fig. S4 showed that there is a spectrum overlap between the excitation of HCDs and the absorption of Au3+, indicating the IFE between HCDs and Au3+ [42,43].

The excellent water solubility, relatively small particle size, high Au3+ sensitivity, and bright PL demonstrate that the HCDs have the important potential for detecting Au3+ in biological systems. The PC12 cells were incubated with HCDs for 12 h at 37 °C, a bright green fluorescence was recorded in Fig. 5 a. As illustrated in Fig. 5b–e, with increasing concentrations of Au3+ in PC12 cell, the fluorescence intensity was decreased step by step. The fluorescence intensities of HCDs in PC12 cells were displayed in Fig. S5, indicating the HCDs possess excellent biocompatibility, which can be successfully used for bioimaging as well as monitoring Au3+ in the living cells. Table S3 indicated that analytical performance of the developed sensor compared with that of some previously published fluorescence probes. The results showed that as-synthesized HCDs for the detection of Au3+ exhibited broad linear range, low detection limit, good ions selectivity, and which could be used for visualizing detection of Au3+ in PC12 cells.

In virtue of the unique PL properties of HCDs, the WLEDs were fabricated by coating CDs/PVA composite film as appeared in experimental section. The fluorescent spectrum of as-fabricated LED was obviously consisted of two emission bands peaked at 420 and 550 nm (Fig. 6 a), the photograph of the as-fabricated LED (inset in Fig. 6a) showed a bright white illumination. Fig. 6b displayed corresponding Commission International d’Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates of (0.29, 0.33), belonging to the white area. The result of above illustrated that the white color LEDs from CDs with dual-peak emission were produced.

In conclusion, HCDs and OCDs were prepared by a simple one-pot solvothermal method. They showed the small and dispersive particle size, bright fluorescence, as well as single exponential characteristic of fluorescent decay. As-prepared HCDs possess excellent water solubility and good biocompatibility, which can be used for highly sensitive and selective sensing Au3+ in aqueous solution and living cells. Furthermore, the HCDs were successfully applied to fabricate the WLEDs with CIE color coordinates of (0.29, 0.33). The applications of OCDs are also being tracked by our team.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21576071, 21776061), the State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals (KF1514), and the program for Science & Technology Innovation Team in Universities of Henan Province (19IRTSTHN029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.024.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wenli Gao studies in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University as a master candidate. Her research focuses on the synthesis and application of fluorescent carbon dots.

Yanmei Zhou received her Ph.D. degree in environmental engineering from Beijing University of Technology in 2006. She is working in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University as a professor now. Her current interests include fluorescent probes, supercapacitor and hydrolysis of cellulose.

Chenggong Xu received his M.S. degree in biosensor from University of Jinan in 2017. He studies in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University as a doctor candidate now. His research focuses on the synthesis and application of fluorescent probe.
Meixia Guo is working in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University now. Her research focuses on environment analysis.
Zhichong Qi is working in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University now. His research focuses on environment analysis.

Xiaojun Peng received his Ph.D. degree from Dalian University of Technology in 1986. He is working in State Key Laboratory, Fine Chemicals of Dalian University of Technology as a professor now. His current interests include fluorescent probe and functional dye.
Bin Gao is working in Department of Agricultural and Biological Engineering, University of Florida as a professor now. His research focuses on environment analysis.
